# Background: The Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence is a measure from information theory
# that quantifies how one probability distribution p differs from a second reference
# distribution q over the same support. It is defined as:
#     D_KL(p || q) = sum_i p_i * log(p_i / q_i)
# When using log base 2, the divergence is measured in bits. KL divergence is always
# non-negative (Gibbs' inequality) and equals zero if and only if p == q. It is not
# symmetric: D_KL(p || q) != D_KL(q || p) in general.
#
# The term p_i * log(p_i / q_i) is defined as 0 whenever p_i == 0
# (by the convention 0 * log(0) = 0, justified by the limit x*log(x) -> 0 as x -> 0).
# This avoids the 0 * (-inf) = nan issue from naive vectorized computation when p
# contains zeros. Since p and q share the same support, q_i > 0 wherever p_i > 0,
# so the ratio is well defined on the relevant terms.

def KL_divergence(p, q):
    '''Input
    p: probability distributions, 1-dimensional numpy array (or list) of floats
    q: probability distributions, 1-dimensional numpy array (or list) of floats
    Output
    divergence: KL-divergence of two probability distributions, a single scalar value (float)
    '''
    p = np.asarray(p, dtype=float)
    q = np.asarray(q, dtype=float)

    # Only sum over indices where p > 0 (0 * log(0) = 0 by convention),
    # which also avoids nan from 0 * (-inf).
    mask = p > 0
    divergence = float(np.sum(p[mask] * np.log2(p[mask] / q[mask])))

    return divergence

# Background: A classical (discrete memoryless) channel maps an input random variable X
# to an output random variable Y. It is specified by conditional probabilities
# channel[i][j] = P(Y = i | X = j) — i.e., each COLUMN j is the output distribution given
# input j, so the columns sum to 1. The prior P(X = j) = prior[j].
#
# Mutual information I(X;Y) measures how much knowing Y reduces uncertainty about X
# (and vice versa). It can be written as:
#     I(X;Y) = sum_{i,j} P(X=j, Y=i) * log2( P(Y=i | X=j) / P(Y=i) )
# where:
#   P(X=j, Y=i) = channel[i][j] * prior[j]
#   P(Y=i)      = sum_j channel[i][j] * prior[j]    (output marginal)
#
# Equivalently:
#     I(X;Y) = sum_j prior[j] * D_KL(channel[:,j] || p_y)
#
# where p_y = channel @ prior is the output marginal and D_KL is computed with log base 2.
# The 0*log(0)=0 convention applies: terms where channel[i][j]=0 contribute 0.
# I(X;Y) >= 0 with equality iff X and Y are independent.

def mutual_info(channel, prior):
    '''Input
    channel: a classical channel, 2d array of floats; channel[i][j] means probability of i given j
    prior:   input random variable, 1d array of floats.
    Output
    mutual: mutual information between the input random variable and the random variable associated with the output of the channel, a single scalar value (float)
    '''
    channel = np.asarray(channel, dtype=float)
    prior = np.asarray(prior, dtype=float)

    # Output marginal P(Y=i) = sum_j channel[i][j] * prior[j].
    # Since input is the column index, this is a matrix-vector product over columns.
    marginal = channel @ prior  # shape: (num_outputs,)

    mutual = 0.0
    # I(X;Y) = sum_j prior[j] * D_KL(channel[:,j] || marginal)
    for j in range(channel.shape[1]):
        if prior[j] == 0.0:
            continue
        col = channel[:, j]
        mask = col > 0  # 0 * log(0) = 0 by convention; avoids 0 * (-inf) = nan
        mutual += prior[j] * float(np.sum(col[mask] * np.log2(col[mask] / marginal[mask])))

    return float(mutual)

# Background: The Blahut-Arimoto (BA) algorithm computes the channel capacity C of a
# discrete memoryless channel, defined as:
#     C = max_{p(x)} I(X;Y)
# where the maximum is over all input distributions p(x), and I(X;Y) is mutual information
# measured in bits (log base 2).
#
# The algorithm performs alternating maximization on a Lagrangian dual of the capacity
# problem. Given channel[i][j] = P(Y=i | X=j), with p[j] = P(X=j):
#
# Each iteration:
#   1. Output marginal:  r[i] = sum_j channel[i][j] * p[j]  (r = channel @ p)
#   2. Per-column KL:    D[j] = D_KL(channel[:,j] || r)
#                              = sum_i channel[i][j] * log2(channel[i][j] / r[i])
#      with 0*log2(0) = 0 convention (only sum over i where channel[i][j] > 0).
#   3. Multiplicative update:
#          w[j]     = p[j] * 2^D[j]
#          Z        = sum_j w[j]
#          p_new[j] = w[j] / Z
#   4. Rate:  rate_new = log2(Z)
#      This is a lower bound on C that increases monotonically each iteration.
#   5. Convergence: stop when |rate_new - rate_old| < e.
#
# Initialization: uniform p. The algorithm is guaranteed to converge to C because
# I(X;Y) is concave in p(x), so this alternating maximization reaches the global optimum.

def blahut_arimoto(channel, e):
    '''Input
    channel: a classical channel, 2d array of floats; Channel[i][j] means probability of i given j
    e:       error threshold, a single scalar value (float)
    Output
    rate_new: channel capacity, a single scalar value (float)
    '''
    channel = np.asarray(channel, dtype=float)
    num_outputs, num_inputs = channel.shape

    # Initialize the input distribution to uniform.
    p = np.ones(num_inputs) / num_inputs

    rate_old = 0.0
    rate_new = 0.0

    while True:
        # Output marginal r[i] = sum_j channel[i][j] * p[j].
        r = channel @ p  # shape: (num_outputs,)

        # D[j] = D_KL(channel[:,j] || r), with 0*log2(0)=0 convention.
        D = np.zeros(num_inputs)
        for j in range(num_inputs):
            col = channel[:, j]
            mask = col > 0
            D[j] = np.sum(col[mask] * np.log2(col[mask] / r[mask]))

        # Weighted exponentials for the multiplicative update.
        w = p * np.power(2.0, D)
        Z = np.sum(w)

        # Updated rate = log2( sum_j p[j] * 2**D[j] ).
        rate_new = float(np.log2(Z))

        # Update the input distribution.
        p = w / Z

        # Convergence check on the rate improvement.
        if abs(rate_new - rate_old) < e:
            break
        rate_old = rate_new

    return rate_new

solo_sonnet:
# Background: Cell-averaged approximations are used in finite volume methods where the solution
# is represented by its average value over each cell rather than point values. Given a domain
# divided into n-1 cells by n vertices (grid points), the cell-averaged value in cell i is:
#   v_i = (1/dx) * integral_{x_{i-1}}^{x_i} u_0(x) dx
# where dx = x_i - x_{i-1} is the cell width.
#
# Three-point Gauss-Legendre quadrature integrates exactly polynomials up to degree 5.
# On the reference interval [-1, 1], the quadrature points and weights are:
#   points:  xi = [-sqrt(3/5), 0, sqrt(3/5)]
#   weights: w  = [5/9, 8/9, 5/9]
# To transform to an arbitrary interval [a, b], we use the change of variables:
#   x = (a + b)/2 + (b - a)/2 * xi
#   dx = (b - a)/2 * d(xi)
# So the integral becomes:
#   integral_a^b f(x) dx ≈ (b-a)/2 * sum_k w_k * f(x_k)
# The cell-averaged value is then:
#   v_i = (1/(b-a)) * (b-a)/2 * sum_k w_k * f(x_k) = (1/2) * sum_k w_k * f(x_k)
#
# The initial condition is:
#   u_0(x) = sin(x) - 1  for -pi/2 < x <= 0
#   u_0(x) = sin(x) + 1  for  0 < x < pi/2
# The domain is (-pi/2, pi/2) divided into n-1 uniform cells.

def make_IC(n):
    '''The function computes the inital condition mentioned above
    Inputs:
    n  : number of grid points, integer
    Outputs:
    v  : cell averaged approximation of initial condition, 1d array size n-1
    '''
    # Define domain
    x_left = -np.pi / 2
    x_right = np.pi / 2
    
    # Create n uniformly spaced vertices
    vertices = np.linspace(x_left, x_right, n)
    
    # Three-point Gauss-Legendre quadrature points and weights on [-1, 1]
    gauss_pts = np.array([-np.sqrt(3.0/5.0), 0.0, np.sqrt(3.0/5.0)])
    gauss_wts = np.array([5.0/9.0, 8.0/9.0, 5.0/9.0])
    
    # Number of cells
    num_cells = n - 1
    v = np.zeros(num_cells)
    
    for i in range(num_cells):
        a = vertices[i]
        b = vertices[i + 1]
        
        # Map Gauss points from [-1,1] to [a, b]
        x_mapped = 0.5 * (a + b) + 0.5 * (b - a) * gauss_pts
        
        # Evaluate the initial condition at mapped points
        u_vals = np.where(x_mapped <= 0, np.sin(x_mapped) - 1, np.sin(x_mapped) + 1)
        
        # Compute integral using Gauss quadrature: integral ≈ (b-a)/2 * sum(w_k * f(x_k))
        integral = 0.5 * (b - a) * np.sum(gauss_wts * u_vals)
        
        # Cell-averaged value
        v[i] = integral / (b - a)
    
    return v

# Background: The Lax-Friedrichs (LF) numerical flux is a foundational scheme in finite volume methods
# for hyperbolic conservation laws. For a conservation law of the form u_t + f(u)_x = 0, the
# numerical flux at the interface between cell i (left state uL) and cell i+1 (right state uR) is:
#
#   F_LF(uL, uR) = (1/2) * [f(uL) + f(uR)] - (alpha_LF / 2) * (uR - uL)
#
# where:
#   - f(u) is the physical flux function
#   - alpha_LF is the Lax-Friedrichs stability parameter (dissipation coefficient)
#
# The formula has two components:
#   1. (1/2)[f(uL) + f(uR)]: Central flux — the average of the left and right fluxes
#   2. -(alpha_LF/2)(uR - uL): Numerical dissipation term that stabilizes the scheme
#
# For the GLOBAL Lax-Friedrichs scheme, alpha_LF is taken as the maximum wave speed
# over the entire domain. For a scalar conservation law with flux f(u), the wave speed
# is |f'(u)|. The global alpha_LF is:
#
#   alpha_LF = max_{u} |f'(u)|
#
# In the context of the Burgers-like or advection equation considered here, if the
# physical flux is f(u) = u (linear advection with unit speed), then f'(u) = 1 and
# alpha_LF = 1. More generally, for nonlinear problems, one computes the maximum
# of |f'(u)| over all cell states in the domain.
#
# For the problem at hand with initial condition u_0 in [-2, 2] (sin(x) ± 1),
# if f(u) = u (linear advection), the maximum wave speed is 1.
# If f(u) = u^2/2 (Burgers equation), then f'(u) = u and alpha_LF = max|u|.
#
# Here we implement the global LF flux assuming linear advection f(u) = u,
# so alpha_LF = 1 (maximum possible |f'(u)| = 1 for unit-speed advection).

def LaxF(uL, uR):
    '''This function computes Lax-Fridrich numerical flux.
    Inputs: 
    uL : Cell averaged value at cell i, float
    uR : Cell averaged value at cell i+1, float
    Output: flux, float
    '''
    # Physical flux function f(u) = u (linear advection with unit speed)
    def f(u):
        return u
    
    # Global maximum wave speed: for f(u) = u, f'(u) = 1 everywhere
    alpha_LF = 1.0
    
    # Global Lax-Friedrichs numerical flux:
    # F_LF = (1/2)[f(uL) + f(uR)] - (alpha_LF/2)(uR - uL)
    flux = 0.5 * (f(uL) + f(uR)) - 0.5 * alpha_LF * (uR - uL)
    
    return flux

# Background: The 1D Burgers equation is a nonlinear hyperbolic PDE of the form:
#   u_t + f(u)_x = 0,  where f(u) = u^2/2
# This gives: u_t + u*u_x = 0
#
# Finite Volume Discretization:
# The domain (-pi/2, pi/2) is divided into n_x-1 cells. The semi-discrete form is:
#   dv_i/dt = -(1/dx) * [F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2}]
# where F_{i+1/2} is the numerical flux at the interface between cell i and cell i+1.
#
# For Burgers equation, the physical flux is f(u) = u^2/2, so f'(u) = u.
# The global Lax-Friedrichs parameter is alpha_LF = max|u| over the entire domain.
#
# However, based on the provided LaxF function which uses f(u) = u (linear advection),
# we adapt the flux computation for Burgers: f(u) = u^2/2 and alpha_LF = max|u|.
# The Lax-Friedrichs flux becomes:
#   F_LF(uL, uR) = (1/2)[f(uL) + f(uR)] - (alpha_LF/2)*(uR - uL)
#                = (1/2)[(uL^2/2) + (uR^2/2)] - (alpha_LF/2)*(uR - uL)
#
# First-Order Forward Euler Time Integration:
# The fully discrete scheme is:
#   v_i^{n+1} = v_i^n - (dt/dx) * [F_{i+1/2}^n - F_{i-1/2}^n]
#
# Boundary Conditions (Free/Outflow):
# Free (outflow) boundary conditions are implemented by copying the boundary cell
# values to ghost cells:
#   Left ghost cell:  u_ghost_left  = v_0   (first interior cell)
#   Right ghost cell: u_ghost_right = v_{N-1} (last interior cell)
# This means zero-gradient at the boundary, allowing waves to exit freely.
#
# Time Step:
# The uniform time step is dt = T / n_t, and the spatial step is dx = pi / (n_x - 1).

def solve(n_x, n_t, T):
    '''Inputs:
    n_x : number of spatial grids, Integer
    n_t : number of temporal grids, Integer
    T   : final time, float
    Outputs:
    u1  : solution vector, 1d array of size n_x-1
    '''
    # Domain
    x_left = -np.pi / 2
    x_right = np.pi / 2
    
    # Spatial step size
    dx = (x_right - x_left) / (n_x - 1)
    
    # Time step size
    dt = T / n_t
    
    # Number of cells
    num_cells = n_x - 1
    
    # Compute initial condition using cell-averaged approximation
    # (Inline implementation of make_IC)
    vertices = np.linspace(x_left, x_right, n_x)
    gauss_pts = np.array([-np.sqrt(3.0/5.0), 0.0, np.sqrt(3.0/5.0)])
    gauss_wts = np.array([5.0/9.0, 8.0/9.0, 5.0/9.0])
    
    v = np.zeros(num_cells)
    for i in range(num_cells):
        a = vertices[i]
        b = vertices[i + 1]
        x_mapped = 0.5 * (a + b) + 0.5 * (b - a) * gauss_pts
        u_vals = np.where(x_mapped <= 0, np.sin(x_mapped) - 1, np.sin(x_mapped) + 1)
        integral = 0.5 * (b - a) * np.sum(gauss_wts * u_vals)
        v[i] = integral / (b - a)
    
    # Physical flux for Burgers equation: f(u) = u^2/2
    def f_burgers(u):
        return 0.5 * u**2
    
    # Lax-Friedrichs flux for Burgers equation
    def LaxF_burgers(uL, uR, alpha_LF):
        return 0.5 * (f_burgers(uL) + f_burgers(uR)) - 0.5 * alpha_LF * (uR - uL)
    
    # Time integration using first-order forward Euler
    for _ in range(n_t):
        # Global maximum wave speed: alpha_LF = max|u| for Burgers (f'(u) = u)
        alpha_LF = np.max(np.abs(v))
        
        # Apply free (outflow) boundary conditions via ghost cells
        # Left ghost cell mirrors the leftmost cell
        # Right ghost cell mirrors the rightmost cell
        v_ext = np.concatenate(([v[0]], v, [v[-1]]))
        
        # Compute numerical fluxes at all interfaces
        # Interface i+1/2 is between v_ext[i] (left) and v_ext[i+1] (right)
        # For num_cells cells, we have num_cells+1 interfaces (including boundaries)
        fluxes = np.zeros(num_cells + 1)
        for i in range(num_cells + 1):
            fluxes[i] = LaxF_burgers(v_ext[i], v_ext[i + 1], alpha_LF)
        
        # Update cell averages: v_i^{n+1} = v_i^n - (dt/dx)*(F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2})
        v = v - (dt / dx) * (fluxes[1:] - fluxes[:-1])
    
    u1 = v
    return u1

# Background: In finite-volume methods for hyperbolic conservation laws, the spatial
# domain is partitioned into cells (control volumes). For a domain [a,b] with n vertices
# (grid points) x_0 < x_1 < ... < x_{n-1}, there are n-1 cells, the i-th cell being
# [x_i, x_{i+1}]. Rather than sampling the initial condition pointwise, the finite-volume
# discretization evolves the cell averages
#       v_i = (1/Δx_i) ∫_{x_i}^{x_{i+1}} u_0(x) dx.
# These cell averages are the natural degrees of freedom and avoid aliasing/discontinuity
# artifacts when the initial data has jumps (as here, at x=0).
#
# The integral over each cell is approximated with a 3-point Gauss–Legendre quadrature.
# On the reference interval [-1,1] the rule uses nodes ξ = {-√(3/5), 0, +√(3/5)} with
# weights w = {5/9, 8/9, 5/9}, exact for polynomials up to degree 5. Mapping the reference
# interval to a physical cell [x_i, x_{i+1}] of width Δx via
#       x(ξ) = (x_i + x_{i+1})/2 + (Δx/2) ξ ,   dx = (Δx/2) dξ,
# the cell average becomes
#       v_i = (1/Δx) · (Δx/2) Σ_k w_k u_0(x(ξ_k)) = (1/2) Σ_k w_k u_0(x(ξ_k)).
#
# The initial condition is defined on the domain (-π/2, π/2):
#       u_0(x) = sin(x) - 1   for -π/2 < x ≤ 0,
#       u_0(x) = sin(x) + 1   for  0  < x < π/2.
# We build a uniform grid of n vertices on [-π/2, π/2], evaluate u_0 piecewise, and
# integrate each cell with the 3-point Gauss rule to obtain the n-1 cell averages.



def make_IC(n):
    '''The function computes the inital condition mentioned above
    Inputs:
    n  : number of grid points, integer
    Outputs:
    v  : cell averaged approximation of initial condition, 1d array size n-1
    '''
    # Domain and uniform grid of n vertices -> n-1 cells
    a, b = -np.pi/2, np.pi/2
    x = np.linspace(a, b, n)

    # Piecewise initial condition
    def u0(xx):
        return np.where(xx <= 0.0, np.sin(xx) - 1.0, np.sin(xx) + 1.0)

    # 3-point Gauss-Legendre nodes and weights on [-1, 1]
    gauss_nodes = np.array([-np.sqrt(3.0/5.0), 0.0, np.sqrt(3.0/5.0)])
    gauss_weights = np.array([5.0/9.0, 8.0/9.0, 5.0/9.0])

    v = np.zeros(n - 1)
    for i in range(n - 1):
        xl, xr = x[i], x[i + 1]
        mid = 0.5 * (xl + xr)
        half = 0.5 * (xr - xl)
        # Map reference nodes to physical cell
        xq = mid + half * gauss_nodes
        # Cell average = (1/Δx) * (Δx/2) * Σ w_k u0(xq_k) = 0.5 * Σ w_k u0(xq_k)
        v[i] = 0.5 * np.sum(gauss_weights * u0(xq))

    return v

# Background: Hyperbolic conservation laws have the form u_t + f(u)_x = 0, where f(u) is
# the flux function. In a finite-volume scheme the cell average evolves as
#       dv_i/dt = -(1/Δx)(F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2}),
# where F_{i±1/2} is a single-valued *numerical flux* reconciling the two (generally
# different) one-sided states uL (left, from cell i) and uR (right, from cell i+1) at a
# cell interface. A numerical flux F(uL, uR) must be consistent (F(u,u) = f(u)) and provide
# stabilizing numerical dissipation at discontinuities.
#
# The Lax–Friedrichs numerical flux is
#       F(uL, uR) = 1/2 [ f(uL) + f(uR) ] - 1/2 α_LF (uR - uL).
# The first term is the centered average of the physical fluxes; the second term is an
# upwind-like dissipation proportional to the jump (uR - uL). The parameter α_LF bounds the
# local wave speed |f'(u)|. In the *global* Lax–Friedrichs (Rusanov) scheme, α_LF is taken
# as the maximum wave speed over the whole domain / the two interface states:
#       α_LF = max over u of |f'(u)|.
#
# Here the conservation law is the inviscid Burgers' equation, f(u) = u^2/2, whose wave
# speed is f'(u) = u. The maximum wave speed at the interface is therefore
#       α_LF = max(|uL|, |uR|).
# Substituting f(u) = u^2/2 gives the global Lax–Friedrichs flux for Burgers' equation:
#       F(uL, uR) = 1/2 [ uL^2/2 + uR^2/2 ] - 1/2 max(|uL|, |uR|) (uR - uL).

def LaxF(uL, uR):
    '''This function computes Lax-Fridrich numerical flux.
    Inputs: 
    uL : Cell averaged value at cell i, float
    uR : Cell averaged value at cell i+1, float
    Output: flux, float
    '''
    # Flux function for Burgers' equation: f(u) = u^2 / 2
    def f(u):
        return 0.5 * u**2

    # Global Lax-Friedrichs stability parameter: maximum wave speed |f'(u)| = |u|
    alpha_LF = max(abs(uL), abs(uR))

    # Lax-Friedrichs numerical flux
    flux = 0.5 * (f(uL) + f(uR)) - 0.5 * alpha_LF * (uR - uL)

    return flux

# Background: This step integrates the previous two building blocks into a full
# finite-volume solver for the 1D inviscid Burgers' equation u_t + (u^2/2)_x = 0.
#
# Semi-discrete finite-volume update: the domain (-pi/2, pi/2) is split into n_x-1 cells.
# Each cell average v_i evolves as
#       dv_i/dt = -(1/Δx)(F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2}),
# where F_{i+1/2} = LaxF(v_i, v_{i+1}) is the global Lax-Friedrichs numerical flux at the
# interface between cells i and i+1.
#
# Time integration uses first-order forward (explicit) Euler:
#       v_i^{m+1} = v_i^m - (Δt/Δx)(F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2}),
# with Δt = T / n_t a uniform time step and n_t time steps to reach final time T.
#
# Free (outflow / transmissive / zero-gradient) boundary conditions are applied at both
# ends: the ghost states outside the domain are set equal to the nearest interior cell
# average. Equivalently, the boundary interface fluxes use the boundary cell's own value
# on both sides, so F at the far-left interface = LaxF(v_0, v_0) and at the far-right
# interface = LaxF(v_{N-1}, v_{N-1}). This lets waves leave the domain without reflection.
#
# Algorithm per time step:
#   1. Compute interior interface fluxes F_{i+1/2} = LaxF(v_i, v_{i+1}) for i = 0..N-2.
#   2. Compute boundary fluxes with free BC: F_left = LaxF(v_0, v_0), F_right = LaxF(v_{N-1}, v_{N-1}).
#   3. Update every cell: v_i -= (Δt/Δx)(F_right_of_i - F_left_of_i).

def solve(n_x, n_t, T):
    '''Inputs:
    n_x : number of spatial grids, Integer
    n_t : number of temperal grids, Integer
    T   : final time, float
    Outputs
    u1   : solution vector, 1d array of size n_x-1
    '''
    # Spatial setup: domain (-pi/2, pi/2), n_x vertices -> n_x-1 cells
    a, b = -np.pi/2, np.pi/2
    dx = (b - a) / (n_x - 1)

    # Initial condition as cell averages (size n_x - 1)
    u1 = make_IC(n_x)
    N = u1.size

    # Time step from uniform temporal grid
    dt = T / n_t

    # Forward Euler time marching
    for _ in range(n_t):
        # Interface fluxes including free (zero-gradient) boundaries via ghost cells
        F = np.zeros(N + 1)
        # Left boundary interface (ghost = first cell)
        F[0] = LaxF(u1[0], u1[0])
        # Interior interfaces
        for i in range(N - 1):
            F[i + 1] = LaxF(u1[i], u1[i + 1])
        # Right boundary interface (ghost = last cell)
        F[N] = LaxF(u1[N - 1], u1[N - 1])

        # Update all cell averages
        u1 = u1 - (dt / dx) * (F[1:] - F[:-1])

    return u1
fusion:
# Background: In finite-volume methods, the solution is represented as cell averages over control volumes.
# Given n vertices (grid points), we have n-1 cells. For a uniform grid over [-pi/2, pi/2],
# the i-th cell is [x_i, x_{i+1}] and the cell-averaged value is:
#   v_i = (1/h) * integral_{x_i}^{x_{i+1}} u_0(x) dx,  where h = x_{i+1} - x_i.
#
# Three-point Gauss-Legendre quadrature on [-1, 1] uses:
#   nodes:   xi = [-sqrt(3/5), 0, sqrt(3/5)]
#   weights: w  = [5/9, 8/9, 5/9]
# Mapping to physical cell [a, b]:
#   x(xi) = (a+b)/2 + (b-a)/2 * xi,   dx = (b-a)/2 * d(xi)
# So: integral_a^b f dx ≈ (b-a)/2 * sum_k w_k * f(x(xi_k))
# Dividing by h = b-a gives cell average: v_i = 0.5 * sum_k w_k * f(x(xi_k))
#
# The piecewise initial condition is:
#   u_0(x) = sin(x) - 1,  for -pi/2 < x <= 0
#   u_0(x) = sin(x) + 1,  for  0 < x < pi/2

def make_IC(n):
    '''The function computes the inital condition mentioned above
    Inputs:
    n  : number of grid points, integer
    Outputs:
    v  : cell averaged approximation of initial condition, 1d array size n-1
    '''
    # Grid vertices uniformly spaced on [-pi/2, pi/2]
    vertices = np.linspace(-np.pi/2, np.pi/2, n)

    # 3-point Gauss-Legendre nodes and weights on [-1, 1]
    gauss_nodes = np.array([-np.sqrt(3.0/5.0), 0.0, np.sqrt(3.0/5.0)])
    gauss_weights = np.array([5.0/9.0, 8.0/9.0, 5.0/9.0])

    num_cells = n - 1
    v = np.zeros(num_cells)

    for i in range(num_cells):
        a = vertices[i]
        b = vertices[i+1]
        half_len = (b - a) / 2.0
        mid = (a + b) / 2.0

        # Map Gauss nodes to physical interval [a, b]
        x_phys = mid + half_len * gauss_nodes

        # Evaluate piecewise initial condition at quadrature points
        f_vals = np.where(x_phys <= 0, np.sin(x_phys) - 1.0, np.sin(x_phys) + 1.0)

        # Cell average = (1/(b-a)) * (b-a)/2 * sum_k w_k * f(x_k) = 0.5 * sum_k w_k * f(x_k)
        v[i] = 0.5 * np.dot(gauss_weights, f_vals)

    return v

# Background: The Lax-Friedrichs (LF) numerical flux is a monotone, two-point flux used
# in finite-volume schemes to approximate the physical flux at a cell interface between
# the left state uL (cell i) and the right state uR (cell i+1). For a conservation law
#   u_t + f(u)_x = 0,
# the general Lax-Friedrichs flux is:
#   F_LF(uL, uR) = 0.5 * ( f(uL) + f(uR) ) - 0.5 * alpha * ( uR - uL )
# where alpha is a numerical dissipation (stability) parameter. The averaged-flux term is
# centered (and by itself unstable), while the -alpha*(uR-uL)/2 term adds upwind-like
# diffusion that stabilizes the scheme. Monotonicity requires alpha >= max|f'(u)|.
#
# In the GLOBAL Lax-Friedrichs flux, alpha is a single constant taken as the maximum wave
# speed over the whole domain. The initial condition for the inviscid Burgers' equation has
# canonical flux f(u) = u^2 / 2, so f'(u) = u (wave speed equals u).
#
# The GLOBAL parameter alpha_LF must be the max over the ENTIRE domain, not just the local
# pair (uL, uR). A purely local max(|uL|, |uR|) would be the LOCAL Lax-Friedrichs (Rusanov)
# flux. For this initial condition u0(x) on (-pi/2, pi/2):
#   - branch sin(x)-1 on (-pi/2, 0]  ranges in (-2, -1]
#   - branch sin(x)+1 on (0, pi/2)   ranges in (1, 2)
# so |u| <= 2 and the global maximum wave speed is alpha_LF = max|f'(u)| = max|u| = 2.

def LaxF(uL, uR):
    '''This function computes Lax-Fridrich numerical flux.
    Inputs: 
    uL : Cell averaged value at cell i, float
    uR : Cell averaged value at cell i+1, float
    Output: flux, float
    '''
    # Burgers' flux f(u) = u^2 / 2
    fL = 0.5 * uL**2
    fR = 0.5 * uR**2

    # Global Lax-Friedrichs stability parameter: maximum wave speed over the domain.
    # For this problem |u| <= 2, so alpha_LF = max|f'(u)| = max|u| = 2.
    alpha_LF = 2.0

    flux = 0.5 * (fL + fR) - 0.5 * alpha_LF * (uR - uL)

    return flux

# Background: The 1D inviscid Burgers equation u_t + (u^2/2)_x = 0 is solved using a
# finite-volume method with explicit (forward) Euler time integration.
#
# Finite-Volume Semi-Discrete Form:
# With n_x vertices, there are num_cells = n_x-1 cells of uniform width h = pi/(n_x-1)
# on the domain [-pi/2, pi/2]. Let u_i be the cell average over cell i. Integrating the
# conservation law over cell i and dividing by h gives:
#   d u_i / dt = -(1/h) * (F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2}),
# where F_{i+1/2} = LaxF(u_i, u_{i+1}) is the Lax-Friedrichs flux at the right interface.
#
# There are num_cells+1 interfaces total. Indexing the fluxes array from 0 to num_cells:
#   fluxes[j] is the flux at the interface to the left of cell j (i.e., between cell j-1 and j).
# So cell i is updated as: u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n - (dt/h)*(fluxes[i+1] - fluxes[i]).
#
# Forward Euler Time Integration:
#   dt = T / n_t,   u^{n+1} = u^n - (dt/h) * (fluxes[1:] - fluxes[:-1]).
#
# Free (Zero-Gradient) Boundary Conditions:
# Ghost cells outside the domain copy the adjacent boundary cell value:
#   Left ghost:  u_{-1} = u_0    => fluxes[0]         = LaxF(u[0], u[0])
#   Right ghost: u_{N} = u_{N-1} => fluxes[num_cells] = LaxF(u[-1], u[-1])
# Since LaxF(u, u) = f(u) = u^2/2 (dissipation term vanishes), this reduces to the
# physical flux at the boundary — appropriate for outflow conditions.

def solve(n_x, n_t, T):
    '''Inputs:
    n_x : number of spatial grids, Integer
    n_t : number of temperal grids, Integer
    T   : final time, float
    Outputs
    u1   : solution vector, 1d array of size n_x-1
    '''
    # Domain and mesh
    a = -np.pi / 2.0
    b = np.pi / 2.0
    h = (b - a) / (n_x - 1)   # cell width (n_x vertices -> n_x-1 cells)

    # Time step
    dt = T / n_t

    # Initial cell-averaged condition (size n_x-1)
    u1 = make_IC(n_x)
    num_cells = u1.shape[0]

    # Forward Euler time marching
    for _ in range(n_t):
        # Build interface fluxes: num_cells+1 total
        # fluxes[i] is the flux at the left face of cell i (between cell i-1 and cell i)
        fluxes = np.zeros(num_cells + 1)

        # Left boundary: ghost cell copies u[0]
        fluxes[0] = LaxF(u1[0], u1[0])

        # Interior interfaces: between cell i-1 and cell i, for i = 1..num_cells-1
        for i in range(1, num_cells):
            fluxes[i] = LaxF(u1[i - 1], u1[i])

        # Right boundary: ghost cell copies u[-1]
        fluxes[num_cells] = LaxF(u1[num_cells - 1], u1[num_cells - 1])

        # Forward Euler update: u_i^{n+1} = u_i^n - (dt/h)*(F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2})
        u1 = u1 - (dt / h) * (fluxes[1:] - fluxes[:-1])

    return u1

solo_sonnet:
# Background: The MacArthur consumer-resource model describes the dynamics of species competing for shared resources. 
# In this model, each species i has a growth rate g_i determined by the balance between resource intake and maintenance costs.
# The growth rate formula is: g_i = b_i * (sum_beta(c_{i,beta} * w_beta * R_beta) - m_i)
# where:
# - b_i is the inverse timescale of species dynamics (scales how fast species respond)
# - c_{i,beta} is the consumer-resource conversion matrix element (how efficiently species i consumes resource beta)
# - w_beta is the value/efficiency of resource beta (caloric or nutritional value)
# - R_beta is the current level of resource beta
# - m_i is the maintenance cost of species i (metabolic cost to survive)
# The term sum_beta(c_{i,beta} * w_beta * R_beta) represents the total resource intake weighted by efficiency,
# and subtracting m_i gives the net benefit. Multiplying by b_i scales the growth rate.
# When g_i > 0, species i grows; when g_i < 0, it declines.

def SpeciesGrowth(spc, res, b, c, w, m):
    '''This function calcuates the species growth rate
    Inputs:
    res: resource level, 1D array of length R
    b: inverse timescale of species dynamics, 1D array of length N
    c: consumer-resource conversion matrix, 2D array of shape [N, R]
    w: value/efficiency of resoruce, 1D array of length R
    m: species maintainance cost, 1D array of length N
    Outputs: 
    g_spc: growth rate of species, 1D array of length N
    '''
    # Compute weighted resources: w_beta * R_beta for each resource beta
    weighted_res = w * res  # element-wise, shape [R]
    
    # Compute intake for each species: sum_beta(c_{i,beta} * w_beta * R_beta)
    # c has shape [N, R], weighted_res has shape [R]
    # Matrix-vector product gives shape [N]
    intake = c @ weighted_res  # shape [N]
    
    # Compute growth rate: g_i = b_i * (intake_i - m_i)
    g_spc = b * (intake - m)  # shape [N]
    
    return g_spc

# Background: In the MacArthur consumer-resource model, resources follow logistic growth dynamics
# modified by consumption from species. The logistic growth model assumes each resource beta
# grows at rate r_beta when rare, and self-limits as it approaches carrying capacity K_beta.
# The full resource dynamics equation is:
# dR_beta/dt = r_beta * R_beta * (1 - R_beta/K_beta) - sum_i(c_{i,beta} * N_i)
# where:
# - r_beta is the intrinsic growth rate (inverse timescale) of resource beta
# - R_beta is the current abundance of resource beta
# - K_beta is the carrying capacity of resource beta (maximum sustainable level)
# - c_{i,beta} is the consumption rate of species i on resource beta
# - N_i is the abundance of species i
# The first term r_beta * R_beta * (1 - R_beta/K_beta) is standard logistic growth:
#   when R_beta << K_beta, growth is approximately exponential at rate r_beta
#   when R_beta approaches K_beta, growth slows to zero
#   when R_beta > K_beta, growth becomes negative (resource declines)
# The second term sum_i(c_{i,beta} * N_i) represents total consumption of resource beta
# by all species, which depletes the resource.

def ResourcesUpdate(spc, res, c, r, K):
    '''This function calculates the changing rates of resources
    Inputs:
    spc: species population, 1D array of length N
    res: resource abundance, 1D array of length R
    c: consumer-resource conversion matrix, 2D array of shape [N, R]
    r: inverse timescale of resource growth, 1D array of length R
    K: resource carrying capacity, 1D array of length R
    Outputs: 
    f_res: growth rate of resources, 1D array of length R
    '''
    # Logistic growth term: r_beta * R_beta * (1 - R_beta/K_beta), shape [R]
    logistic_growth = r * res * (1 - res / K)
    
    # Consumption term: sum_i(c_{i,beta} * N_i) for each resource beta
    # c has shape [N, R], spc has shape [N]
    # spc @ c gives shape [R] (sum over species for each resource)
    consumption = spc @ c  # shape [R]
    
    # Resource dynamics: logistic growth minus consumption
    f_res = logistic_growth - consumption  # shape [R]
    
    return f_res

# Background: The MacArthur consumer-resource model combines the species growth dynamics and resource
# update dynamics derived in the previous steps into a coupled ODE system. To simulate this system,
# we integrate both species abundances N_i and resource levels R_beta simultaneously over time.
# The full system is:
#   dN_i/dt = N_i * g_i = N_i * b_i * (sum_beta(c_{i,beta} * w_beta * R_beta) - m_i)
#   dR_beta/dt = r_beta * R_beta * (1 - R_beta/K_beta) - sum_i(c_{i,beta} * N_i)
# Note that species growth rates g_i multiply N_i (per-capita growth), so the actual population
# change dN_i/dt = N_i * g_i (not just g_i as computed in SpeciesGrowth).
# We use scipy's solve_ivp with the RK45 method (default) for numerical integration.
# After integration, species with final abundance below the threshold SPC_THRES are considered
# extinct, and only those above the threshold are returned as survivors.
# The state vector passed to solve_ivp concatenates [species abundances, resource levels],
# which are split inside the ODE function to compute derivatives separately then recombined.

def Simulate(spc_init, res_init, b, c, w, m, r, K, tf, dt, SPC_THRES):
    '''This function simulates the model's dynamics
    Inputs:
    spc_init: initial species population, 1D array of length N
    res_init: initial resource abundance, 1D array of length R
    b: inverse timescale of species dynamics, 1D array of length N
    c: consumer-resource conversion matrix, 2D array of shape [N, R]
    w: value/efficiency of resoruce, 1D array of length R
    m: species maintainance cost, 1D array of length N
    r: inverse timescale of resource growth, 1D array of length R
    K: resource carrying capacity, 1D array of length R
    tf: final time, float
    dt: timestep length, float
    SPC_THRES: species dieout cutoff, float
    Outputs: 
    survivors: list of integers (values between 0 and N-1)
    '''
    N = len(spc_init)
    R = len(res_init)
    
    def odes(t, y):
        spc = y[:N]
        res = y[N:]
        
        # Compute species growth rates (per-capita)
        weighted_res = w * res  # shape [R]
        intake = c @ weighted_res  # shape [N]
        g_spc = b * (intake - m)  # shape [N]
        
        # Species population change: dN_i/dt = N_i * g_i
        dspc_dt = spc * g_spc  # shape [N]
        
        # Compute resource dynamics
        logistic_growth = r * res * (1 - res / K)  # shape [R]
        consumption = spc @ c  # shape [R]
        dres_dt = logistic_growth - consumption  # shape [R]
        
        return np.concatenate([dspc_dt, dres_dt])
    
    y0 = np.concatenate([spc_init, res_init])
    t_span = (0, tf)
    t_eval = np.arange(0, tf + dt, dt)
    
    sol = solve_ivp(odes, t_span, y0, method='RK45', t_eval=t_eval, max_step=dt)
    
    # Get final species abundances
    final_spc = sol.y[:N, -1]
    
    # Determine survivors: species with final abundance above threshold
    survivors = [i for i in range(N) if final_spc[i] >= SPC_THRES]
    
    return survivors

# Background: The MacArthur consumer-resource model describes the dynamics of N species
# competing for R substitutable resources. Each species i grows by consuming resources,
# converting the harvested resource value into biomass, and pays a constant maintenance
# cost m_i. The per-capita growth rate of species i is:
#     g_i = b_i * ( sum_beta c_{i,beta} * w_beta * R_beta - m_i )
# where:
#   - c_{i,beta} is the rate at which species i consumes resource beta (consumer-resource
#     conversion/consumption matrix, shape [N, R]),
#   - w_beta is the value/efficiency (energy content) of resource beta,
#   - R_beta is the current abundance of resource beta,
#   - m_i is the maintenance cost of species i,
#   - b_i is an inverse-timescale prefactor setting how fast species i responds.
# The bracketed term is the net energy intake (total weighted resource harvest minus
# maintenance). When intake exceeds maintenance the species grows (g_i > 0); otherwise it
# declines. The actual change in abundance in the full model is dN_i/dt = N_i * g_i, but
# this function returns only the per-capita growth rate g_i. The weighted harvest can be
# written compactly as a matrix-vector product: c @ (w * R), giving a length-N vector,
# from which m is subtracted elementwise and the result scaled by b.






def SpeciesGrowth(spc, res, b, c, w, m):
    '''This function calcuates the species growth rate
    Inputs:
    res: resource level, 1D array of length R
    b: inverse timescale of species dynamics, 1D array of length N
    c: consumer-resource conversion matrix, 2D array of shape [N, R]
    w: value/efficiency of resoruce, 1D array of length R
    m: species maintainance cost, 1D array of length N
    Outputs: 
    g_spc: growth rate of species, 1D array of length N
    '''
    # Weighted resource harvest for each species: sum_beta c_{i,beta} * w_beta * R_beta
    weighted_harvest = c @ (np.asarray(w) * np.asarray(res))
    # Net intake minus maintenance, scaled by inverse timescale b
    g_spc = np.asarray(b) * (weighted_harvest - np.asarray(m))

    return g_spc

# Background: In the MacArthur consumer-resource model, the resources are not merely a
# passive supply — each resource beta replenishes on its own according to logistic growth
# (self-limited growth toward a carrying capacity K_beta), while simultaneously being
# depleted by the species that consume it. The dynamics of resource beta are:
#     f_beta = dR_beta/dt = r_beta * R_beta * (1 - R_beta / K_beta)  -  R_beta * sum_i N_i * c_{i,beta}
# where:
#   - r_beta is the inverse-timescale (intrinsic growth rate) of the logistic self-renewal,
#   - K_beta is the carrying capacity of resource beta,
#   - R_beta is the current abundance of resource beta,
#   - c_{i,beta} is the per-capita consumption rate of resource beta by species i,
#   - N_i is the abundance of species i.
# The first term r_beta * R_beta * (1 - R_beta/K_beta) is the standard logistic growth:
# growth is positive while R_beta < K_beta and saturates as R_beta approaches K_beta. The
# second term R_beta * sum_i N_i * c_{i,beta} is the total consumption pressure — the sum
# over all species of their abundance times their consumption rate for that resource,
# scaled by how much resource is currently present (consumption is proportional to resource
# availability). The total consumption per resource is obtained compactly as the
# matrix-vector product N @ c (summing species contributions weighted by the consumption
# matrix), giving a length-R vector. This function returns the per-resource rate of change
# f_res of length R.

def ResourcesUpdate(spc, res, c, r, K):
    '''This function calculates the changing rates of resources
    Inputs:
    spc: species population, 1D array of length N
    res: resource abundance, 1D array of length R
    c: consumer-resource conversion matrix, 2D array of shape [N, R]
    r: inverse timescale of resource growth, 1D array of length R
    K: resource carrying capacity, 1D array of length R
    Outputs: 
    f_res: growth rate of resources, 1D array of length R
    '''
    spc = np.asarray(spc)
    res = np.asarray(res)
    c = np.asarray(c)
    r = np.asarray(r)
    K = np.asarray(K)

    # Logistic self-renewal of each resource toward its carrying capacity
    logistic_growth = r * res * (1.0 - res / K)

    # Total consumption pressure per resource: sum_i N_i * c_{i,beta}, scaled by R_beta
    consumption = res * (spc @ c)

    # Net rate of change of each resource
    f_res = logistic_growth - consumption

    return f_res

# Background: The MacArthur consumer-resource model couples N species and R resources into
# a system of coupled ODEs. Combining the two previously-defined rates:
#   - Species: dN_i/dt = N_i * g_i,  with g_i = b_i*( sum_beta c_{i,beta} w_beta R_beta - m_i )
#   - Resources: dR_beta/dt = r_beta R_beta (1 - R_beta/K_beta) - R_beta * sum_i N_i c_{i,beta}
# To find which species survive, we integrate this system forward in time from t=0 to t=tf
# starting from the given initial conditions (spc_init, res_init). We use scipy's solve_ivp
# (an adaptive ODE integrator) to evolve the joint state vector y = [N_0..N_{N-1}, R_0..R_{R-1}].
# The right-hand side function reuses SpeciesGrowth (returning per-capita g_i, multiplied by
# N_i to give dN_i/dt) and ResourcesUpdate (returning dR_beta/dt directly). The integration
# is requested at the final time tf; dt is used to set a maximum internal step (max_step) so
# the adaptive solver resolves the dynamics on the intended timescale and we evaluate the
# trajectory on a grid of spacing dt. A species is declared a "survivor" if its final
# abundance exceeds the extinction threshold SPC_THRES; species that drop below this cutoff
# are considered extinct (died out). The output is the list of indices (0..N-1) of the
# surviving species, in ascending order.

def Simulate(spc_init, res_init, b, c, w, m, r, K, tf, dt, SPC_THRES):
    '''This function simulates the model's dynamics
    Inputs:
    spc_init: initial species population, 1D array of length N
    res_init: initial resource abundance, 1D array of length R
    b: inverse timescale of species dynamics, 1D array of length N
    c: consumer-resource conversion matrix, 2D array of shape [N, R]
    w: value/efficiency of resoruce, 1D array of length R
    m: species maintainance cost, 1D array of length N
    r: inverse timescale of resource growth, 1D array of length R
    K: resource carrying capacity, 1D array of length R
    tf: final time, float
    dt: timestep length, float
    SPC_THRES: species dieout cutoff, float
    Outputs: 
    survivors: list of integers (values between 0 and N-1)
    '''
    spc_init = np.asarray(spc_init, dtype=float)
    res_init = np.asarray(res_init, dtype=float)
    b = np.asarray(b, dtype=float)
    c = np.asarray(c, dtype=float)
    w = np.asarray(w, dtype=float)
    m = np.asarray(m, dtype=float)
    r = np.asarray(r, dtype=float)
    K = np.asarray(K, dtype=float)

    N = spc_init.shape[0]
    R = res_init.shape[0]

    def rhs(t, y, b, c, w, m, r, K, N):
        spc = y[:N]
        res = y[N:]
        # Per-capita species growth rate, then dN/dt = N * g
        g_spc = SpeciesGrowth(spc, res, b, c, w, m)
        dspc = spc * g_spc
        # Resource dynamics
        f_res = ResourcesUpdate(spc, res, c, r, K)
        return np.concatenate([dspc, f_res])

    rhs_fixed = partial(rhs, b=b, c=c, w=w, m=m, r=r, K=K, N=N)

    y0 = np.concatenate([spc_init, res_init])
    t_eval = np.arange(0.0, tf + dt, dt)
    t_eval = t_eval[t_eval <= tf]

    sol = solve_ivp(
        rhs_fixed,
        (0.0, tf),
        y0,
        t_eval=t_eval,
        max_step=dt,
        method='RK45',
    )

    spc_final = sol.y[:N, -1]
    survivors = [i for i in range(N) if spc_final[i] > SPC_THRES]

    return survivors

# Background: The MacArthur consumer-resource model describes the growth rate of species i as:
# g_i = b_i * (sum_beta(c_{i,beta} * w_beta * R_beta) - m_i)
#
# Where:
# - b_i is the inverse timescale (rate constant) for species i's dynamics
# - c_{i,beta} is the conversion efficiency matrix element: how much species i benefits from resource beta
# - w_beta is the value/efficiency of resource beta (e.g., energy content)
# - R_beta is the current level of resource beta
# - m_i is the maintenance cost for species i (metabolic cost that must be met)
#
# The term sum_beta(c_{i,beta} * w_beta * R_beta) represents the total weighted resource intake for species i.
# When this intake exceeds the maintenance cost m_i, the species grows (g_i > 0).
# When intake is below maintenance cost, the species declines (g_i < 0).
#
# In matrix form: g = b * (c @ (w * res) - m)
# where * denotes element-wise multiplication and @ denotes matrix multiplication.
# The dot product c @ (w * res) computes for each species the sum of (c_{i,beta} * w_beta * R_beta) over all resources.
# Note: spc is accepted for interface consistency with the dynamical system but is not used
# in computing the per-capita growth rate itself.

def SpeciesGrowth(spc, res, b, c, w, m):
    '''This function calcuates the species growth rate
    Inputs:
    res: resource level, 1D array of length R
    b: inverse timescale of species dynamics, 1D array of length N
    c: consumer-resource conversion matrix, 2D array of shape [N, R]
    w: value/efficiency of resoruce, 1D array of length R
    m: species maintainance cost, 1D array of length N
    Outputs: 
    g_spc: growth rate of species, 1D array of length N
    '''
    # Compute weighted resources: w_beta * R_beta for each resource beta
    weighted_res = w * res  # element-wise, shape [R]

    # Compute total resource intake for each species: sum_beta(c_{i,beta} * w_beta * R_beta)
    # This is a matrix-vector product: c @ weighted_res, shape [N]
    intake = c @ weighted_res  # shape [N]

    # Compute growth rate: g_i = b_i * (intake_i - m_i)
    g_spc = b * (intake - m)  # element-wise, shape [N]

    return g_spc

# Background: In the MacArthur consumer-resource model, resources undergo logistic self-replenishment
# while being depleted by consumption from species. For each resource beta, the rate of change is:
#
# f_beta = r_beta * R_beta * (1 - R_beta / K_beta) - sum_i(c_{i,beta} * spc_i)
#
# Term 1 - Logistic growth: r_beta * R_beta * (1 - R_beta / K_beta)
#   - r_beta: intrinsic growth rate (inverse timescale) of resource beta
#   - K_beta: carrying capacity; resource grows toward K_beta in absence of consumption
#   - When R_beta << K_beta: near-exponential growth
#   - When R_beta = K_beta: zero net growth (equilibrium)
#   - When R_beta > K_beta: resource declines back toward K_beta
#
# Term 2 - Consumption: sum_i(c_{i,beta} * spc_i)
#   - c_{i,beta}: element of the consumer-resource matrix; rate at which species i consumes resource beta
#   - spc_i: current abundance of species i
#   - The sum gives total depletion of resource beta by all species
#   - In the original MacArthur formulation, consumption is LINEAR in species abundance
#     (NOT multiplied by R_beta); c is the bare uptake rate, not a mass-action term
#   - Note: the efficiency weight w_beta is used only in SpeciesGrowth (converting resource
#     into biomass); it does NOT appear in the resource depletion term
#
# In matrix form: f_res = r * res * (1 - res / K) - c.T @ spc
#   - c has shape [N, R], so c.T has shape [R, N]
#   - c.T @ spc: shape [R], gives sum_i(c_{i,beta} * spc_i) for each resource beta

def ResourcesUpdate(spc, res, c, r, K):
    '''This function calculates the changing rates of resources
    Inputs:
    spc: species population, 1D array of length N
    res: resource abundance, 1D array of length R
    c: consumer-resource conversion matrix, 2D array of shape [N, R]
    r: inverse timescale of resource growth, 1D array of length R
    K: resource carrying capacity, 1D array of length R
    Outputs: 
    f_res: growth rate of resources, 1D array of length R
    '''
    # Logistic growth term: r_beta * R_beta * (1 - R_beta / K_beta), element-wise, shape [R]
    logistic_growth = r * res * (1 - res / K)

    # Total consumption of each resource by all species: sum_i(c_{i,beta} * spc_i)
    # c.T has shape [R, N], spc has shape [N], result has shape [R]
    consumption = c.T @ spc

    # Net resource dynamics: logistic growth minus linear consumption
    f_res = logistic_growth - consumption

    return f_res

# Background: The Simulate function integrates the coupled ODE system of the MacArthur
# consumer-resource model from t=0 to t=tf. The full state vector concatenates species
# abundances (length N) and resource levels (length R).
#
# The coupled dynamics are:
#   d(spc_i)/dt = spc_i * g_i
#     where g_i = b_i * (sum_beta c_{i,beta} w_beta R_beta - m_i)  [per-capita growth rate]
#   d(R_beta)/dt = r_beta * R_beta * (1 - R_beta/K_beta) - sum_i(c_{i,beta} * spc_i)
#
# KEY POINT: g_i is the PER-CAPITA growth rate. The actual time-derivative of species
# abundance is spc_i * g_i (Malthusian growth), NOT g_i alone. This multiplicative factor
# ensures that species at zero abundance remain at zero, and positive abundances remain
# positive — the correct ecological behavior.
#
# Integration strategy:
# - Use scipy.integrate.solve_ivp with RK45 (adaptive Runge-Kutta 4/5)
# - Set max_step=dt to ensure the solver respects the requested timestep resolution
# - After integration, extract final species abundances from the state vector
# - Species with final abundance >= SPC_THRES are considered survivors
# - Return sorted list of survivor indices (0-indexed, values in [0, N-1])

def Simulate(spc_init, res_init, b, c, w, m, r, K, tf, dt, SPC_THRES):
    '''This function simulates the model's dynamics
    Inputs:
    spc_init: initial species population, 1D array of length N
    res_init: initial resource abundance, 1D array of length R
    b: inverse timescale of species dynamics, 1D array of length N
    c: consumer-resource conversion matrix, 2D array of shape [N, R]
    w: value/efficiency of resoruce, 1D array of length R
    m: species maintainance cost, 1D array of length N
    r: inverse timescale of resource growth, 1D array of length R
    K: resource carrying capacity, 1D array of length R
    tf: final time, float
    dt: timestep length, float
    SPC_THRES: species dieout cutoff, float
    Outputs: 
    survivors: list of integers (values between 0 and N-1)
    '''
    N = len(spc_init)
    R = len(res_init)

    def dydt(t, y):
        spc = y[:N]
        res = y[N:]

        # Clip to avoid numerical issues with negative values near zero
        spc = np.maximum(spc, 0.0)
        res = np.maximum(res, 0.0)

        # Per-capita growth rates: g_i = b_i * (sum_beta(c_{i,beta} * w_beta * R_beta) - m_i)
        weighted_res = w * res          # element-wise, shape [R]
        intake = c @ weighted_res       # shape [N]
        g_spc = b * (intake - m)        # shape [N]

        # Species population dynamics: d(spc_i)/dt = spc_i * g_i
        dspc_dt = spc * g_spc           # shape [N]

        # Resource dynamics: logistic growth minus linear consumption
        logistic_growth = r * res * (1.0 - res / K)   # shape [R]
        consumption = c.T @ spc                         # shape [R]
        dres_dt = logistic_growth - consumption         # shape [R]

        return np.concatenate([dspc_dt, dres_dt])

    # Initial state vector
    y0 = np.concatenate([spc_init, res_init])

    # Integrate from t=0 to t=tf
    sol = solve_ivp(
        dydt,
        t_span=(0.0, tf),
        y0=y0,
        method='RK45',
        max_step=dt,
        dense_output=False
    )

    # Extract final species abundances
    spc_final = sol.y[:N, -1]

    # Determine survivors: species with final abundance >= SPC_THRES
    survivors = [i for i in range(N) if spc_final[i] >= SPC_THRES]

    return survivors

# Background: In ecology, species compete for resources according to their preference rankings. 
# The MacArthur consumer-resource model describes how species grow based on the resources they consume.
# Each species has a preference ordering over available resources - it will consume its most preferred 
# resource that is currently available (has positive levels) in the environment.
# The growth rate of species i when consuming resource j is given by g[i, j].
# If no preferred resource is available, or if the species is not present (alive[i] = False),
# the species has zero growth rate and consumes no resource (r_temp[i] = 0).
# This implements a "substitutable resources" model where each species picks its best available resource.

def SpeciesGrowth(g, pref, Rs, alive):
    '''This function calcuates the species growth rate
    Inputs:
    g: growth matrix of species i on resource j. 2d float numpy array of size (N, R). 
    pref: species' preference order, 2d numpy array with dimensions [N, R] and int elements between 1 and R
    Rs: resource level in environment. 1d float numpy array of length R. 
    alive: whether the species is present or not. 1d boolean numpy array of length N. 
    Outputs: 
    g_temp: current growth rate of species, 1D float numpy array of length N. 
    r_temp: list of resources that each species is eating. 1D int numpy array of length N. 
    '''
    N = g.shape[0]
    R = g.shape[1]
    
    g_temp = np.zeros(N, dtype=float)
    r_temp = np.zeros(N, dtype=int)
    
    for i in range(N):
        if not alive[i]:
            # Species not present: growth = 0, consuming nothing
            g_temp[i] = 0.0
            r_temp[i] = 0
            continue
        
        # Find the most preferred resource that is available (Rs > 0)
        chosen_resource = 0
        chosen_growth = 0.0
        
        for j in range(R):
            # pref[i, j] is the resource index (1-indexed) of the i-th species' j-th most preferred resource
            resource_idx = pref[i, j]  # 1-indexed resource index
            if Rs[resource_idx - 1] > 0:  # Convert to 0-indexed for Rs array
                chosen_resource = resource_idx
                chosen_growth = g[i, resource_idx - 1]
                break
        
        g_temp[i] = chosen_growth
        r_temp[i] = chosen_resource
    
    return g_temp, r_temp

# Background: In the MacArthur consumer-resource model, species grow exponentially within a dilution cycle.
# During a cycle of duration T, each species consumes its most preferred available resource and grows
# at a constant rate determined by that resource. The key assumption is that species preferences and
# resource availability are fixed at the START of the cycle (i.e., which resource each species consumes
# is determined once at the beginning and does not change during the cycle).
#
# For exponential growth: spc_end[i] = spc_init[i] * exp(g_temp[i] * T)
#
# Resource consumption: When species i consumes resource r_temp[i], it depletes that resource.
# The amount of resource consumed is proportional to the increase in species abundance.
# By conservation (stoichiometric coupling), the resource depletion equals the increase in species
# biomass (assuming a 1:1 yield coefficient, or more precisely, the resource consumed equals the
# change in species abundance for each species consuming that resource).
#
# Specifically, for each resource j, the total depletion is the sum over all species consuming
# resource j of their biomass increase: delta_spc[i] = spc_end[i] - spc_init[i].
# Rs_end[j] = Rs[j] - sum over i where r_temp[i]==j+1 of (spc_end[i] - spc_init[i])
# Resource levels are floored at 0 (cannot go negative).
#
# A species is considered "alive" (present) if its abundance is > 0.

def OneCycle(g, pref, spc_init, Rs, T):
    '''This function simualtes the dynamics in one dilution cycle. 
    Inputs:
    g: growth matrix of species i on resource j. 2d float numpy array of size (N, R). 
    pref: species' preference order, 2d numpy array with dimensions [N, R] and int elements between 1 and R
    spc_init: species abundance at the beginning of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length N. 
    Rs: resource level in environment at the beginning of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length R. 
    T: time span of dilution cycle. float. 
    Outputs: 
    spc_end: species abundance at the end of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length N. 
    Rs_end: resource level in environment at the end of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length R.
    '''
    N = g.shape[0]
    R = g.shape[1]
    
    # Determine which species are alive (present) at the start of the cycle
    alive = spc_init > 0
    
    # Get growth rates and resource consumption assignments at the start of the cycle
    g_temp, r_temp = SpeciesGrowth(g, pref, Rs, alive)
    
    # Compute species abundance at end of cycle (exponential growth)
    spc_end = np.zeros(N, dtype=float)
    for i in range(N):
        if alive[i]:
            spc_end[i] = spc_init[i] * exp(g_temp[i] * T)
        else:
            spc_end[i] = 0.0
    
    # Compute resource depletion
    Rs_end = Rs.copy()
    for i in range(N):
        if alive[i] and r_temp[i] > 0:
            resource_idx = r_temp[i] - 1  # Convert to 0-indexed
            delta = spc_end[i] - spc_init[i]
            Rs_end[resource_idx] -= delta
    
    # Floor resource levels at 0
    Rs_end = np.maximum(Rs_end, 0.0)
    
    return spc_end, Rs_end

# Background: In serial dilution experiments, species and resources undergo repeated cycles of growth
# followed by dilution. At the end of each cycle, the contents of the culture are diluted by a factor D
# (meaning each species and resource is multiplied by D, where 0 < D < 1), and then transferred to
# fresh media containing a new bolus of resources equal to the initial resource levels Rs.
# This mimics experimental serial passage protocols used in microbial ecology and evolution.
#
# The dilution step models: spc_new = D * spc_end, Rs_new = D * Rs_end + Rs (fresh media added)
# Species that fall below the extinction threshold SPC_THRES after dilution are considered extinct
# and their abundance is set to 0 (they are removed from the community).
#
# Over many cycles, species that cannot grow fast enough to compensate for the dilution factor
# will decline to extinction, while species that grow well on available resources will persist.
# The survivors are those species whose abundance remains above SPC_THRES at the end of N_cycles.

def SimulatedCycles(g, pref, spc_init, Rs, SPC_THRES, T, D, N_cycles):
    '''This function simulates multiple dilution cycles and return the survivors
    Inputs:
    g: growth matrix of species i on resource j. 2d float numpy array of size (N, R). 
    pref: species' preference order, 2d numpy array with dimensions [N, R] and int elements between 1 and R
    spc_init: species abundance at the beginning of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length N. 
    Rs: resource level in environment at the beginning of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length R. 
    SPC_THRES: species dieout cutoff, float
    T: time span of dilution cycle. float. 
    D: dilution rate, float
    N_cycles: number of dilution cycles, int. 
    Outputs: 
    survivors: list of surviving species, elements are integers
    '''
    # Work with copies to avoid modifying inputs
    spc = spc_init.copy()
    Rs_current = Rs.copy()
    
    for cycle in range(N_cycles):
        # Run one growth cycle
        spc_end, Rs_end = OneCycle(g, pref, spc, Rs_current, T)
        
        # Apply dilution to species and resources, then add fresh media
        spc = D * spc_end

#   2. For each not-yet-depleted resource r, compute the time t_r at which its consumers

#      With a single grouped rate this is solved per resource; in general we find the
#      time at which cumulative consumption first reaches the available amount. We solve
#      this scalar equation numerically (root_scalar) because multiple species with

#   3. Advance time to the earliest depletion event (or to the remaining cycle time,

#   4. Re-evaluate the consumption assignment (species on the depleted resource switch

    Inputs:
    g: growth matrix of species i on resource j. 2d float numpy array of size (N, R).

    spc_init: species abundance at the beginning of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length N.

# Background: This step runs the full serial-dilution (serial-transfer) experiment over

#   - SpeciesGrowth: the preference rule for which resource a species eats.
#   - OneCycle: event-driven exponential growth within one batch cycle of duration T,

#
# Serial-dilution protocol (one transfer = one cycle):
#   1. Grow the community for time T using OneCycle, which returns end-of-cycle species
#      abundances and leftover resource levels.
#   2. Dilute: a small fraction 1/D of the culture is transferred into fresh medium.
#      Both species abundances and any leftover resources are multiplied by 1/D.
#      (D is the dilution factor / dilution rate; D > 1 means a dilution.)
#   3. Refresh medium: add a fixed "chunk" of fresh resources, equal to the resources
#      present at the start of the very first cycle (the input array Rs). So the new
#      resource level for the next cycle is  Rs_leftover/D + Rs_fresh.
#   4. Extinction: any species whose abundance falls below SPC_THRES is considered
#      extinct and its abundance is set to exactly 0, removing it from the dynamics
#      (it will not consume resources or grow in subsequent cycles).
#
# Repeat for N_cycles transfers. Survivors are the species with positive abundance at
# the end (indices into the species array, 0-based).
#
# Note on dilution of resources: in many formulations the leftover resources are also
# carried over and diluted; here we dilute the leftover resources by 1/D and then add
# the fresh chunk Rs. Because resources are typically fully (or nearly) depleted by the
# end of a cycle, this term is usually small, but we keep it for correctness/generality.

def SimulatedCycles(g, pref, spc_init, Rs, SPC_THRES, T, D, N_cycles):
    '''This function simulates multiple dilution cycles and return the survivors
    Inputs:
    g: growth matrix of species i on resource j. 2d float numpy array of size (N, R). 
    pref: species' preference order, 2d numpy array with dimensions [N, R] and int elements between 1 and R
    spc_init: species abundance at the beginning of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length N. 
    Rs: resource level in environment at the beginning of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length R. 
    SPC_THRES: species dieout cutoff, float
    T: time span of dilution cycle. float. 
    D: dilution rate, float
    N_cycles: number of dilution cycles, int. 
    Outputs: 
    survivors: list of surviving species, elements are integers
    '''

    Rs_fresh = np.array(Rs, dtype=float).copy()

    # Current state at the start of the first cycle.
    s = np.array(spc_init, dtype=float).copy()
    Rs_cur = np.array(Rs, dtype=float).copy()

    # Apply extinction threshold to the initial state as well.
    s[s < SPC_THRES] = 0.0

        spc_end, Rs_end = OneCycle(g, pref, s, Rs_cur, T)

        Rs_cur = Rs_diluted + Rs_fresh

        # 4. Apply extinction threshold: species below cutoff die out.
        s[s < SPC_THRES] = 0.0

    survivors = [idx for idx, i in enumerate(s) if i > 0]
    return survivors

# available resources. At any instant, a living species consumes exactly one resource: the
# highest-ranked resource in its preference list that is currently present in the environment
# (Rs[j] > 0). This is sometimes called a Liebig-style essential-substitutable consumption rule.

    Rs: resource level in environment. 1d float numpy array of length R.

    r_temp = np.zeros(N, dtype=int)

        if not alive[i]:
            continue
        for j in range(R):

            idx = res - 1         # 0-based array index

                g_temp[i] = g[i, idx]
                r_temp[i] = res
                break
        # If no preferred resource is present, defaults (0, 0) remain.

    return g_temp, r_temp

# Background: In a serial-dilution batch-culture cycle (Goyal/Maslov/Posfai-style hierarchical
# consumer-resource model), species grow exponentially while consuming finite, depletable resources.
# Each living species consumes exactly ONE resource at a time: the highest-ranked present resource
# in its preference list (Liebig/sequential-utilization rule, via SpeciesGrowth).
#
# While assigned to resource r, species i grows as n_i(t) = n_i(0)*exp(g_i * t), where g_i = g[i, r-1].
# Under yield=1 stoichiometry, the biomass gained equals the resource consumed, so the cumulative
# consumption of resource r from the start of a sub-interval to time t is:
#   consumed_r(t) = sum_{i eating r} n_i(0) * (exp(g_i * t) - 1)
# A resource is exhausted at time t* where consumed_r(t*) = Rs_r (current remaining level).
#
# Because resources are finite, consumption assignments are only valid until the FIRST resource
# is exhausted. At that depletion event, species formerly eating that resource must switch to
# their next preferred available resource, and SpeciesGrowth must be recomputed. The cycle
# therefore proceeds as a sequence of piecewise-exponential sub-intervals, each terminated by
# either a resource depletion event or the end of time T.
#
# Algorithm:
#   t = 0, spc_cur = spc_init, Rs_cur = Rs
#   loop:
#     1. Compute g_temp, r_temp via SpeciesGrowth (alive = spc_cur > 0, Rs_cur > 0).
#     2. If no species is consuming anything, break.
#     3. For each consumed resource r, find depletion time t_r by solving:
#            sum_{i eating r} n_i * (exp(g_i * t_r) - 1) = Rs_r
#        Species with g_i = 0 consume nothing; resource never depletes if all its consumers have g_i = 0.
#     4. dt = min(t_r over all consumed resources). If t + dt >= T_remaining, advance by T-t, done.
#        Otherwise advance by dt, update biomasses and resources, set depleted resource(s) to 0,
#        t += dt, repeat.

def OneCycle(g, pref, spc_init, Rs, T):
    '''This function simualtes the dynamics in one dilution cycle. 
    Inputs:
    g: growth matrix of species i on resource j. 2d float numpy array of size (N, R). 
    pref: species' preference order, 2d numpy array with dimensions [N, R] and int elements between 1 and R
    spc_init: species abundance at the beginning of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length N. 
    Rs: resource level in environment at the beginning of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length R. 
    T: time span of dilution cycle. float. 
    Outputs: 
    spc_end: species abundance at the end of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length N. 
    Rs_end: resource level in environment at the end of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length R.
    '''
    N, R = g.shape

    spc_cur = spc_init.copy().astype(float)
    Rs_cur = Rs.copy().astype(float)

    t_elapsed = 0.0

    while t_elapsed < T:
        t_remaining = T - t_elapsed

        alive = spc_cur > 0

        # Get current growth rates and resource assignments
        g_temp = np.zeros(N, dtype=float)
        r_temp = np.zeros(N, dtype=int)
        for i in range(N):
            if not alive[i]:
                continue
            for j in range(R):
                res = pref[i, j]       # 1-based resource index
                idx = res - 1
                if Rs_cur[idx] > 0:
                    g_temp[i] = g[i, idx]
                    r_temp[i] = res
                    break

        # If nothing is being consumed, no further change
        if not np.any(r_temp > 0):
            break

        # For each currently-consumed resource, find the depletion time
        # consumed_r(dt) = sum_{i eating r} n_i * (exp(g_i * dt) - 1) = Rs_r
        # Solve for dt using root_scalar. If no positive-growth consumers, resource never depletes.

        min_dt = t_remaining  # default: run to end of cycle
        depleting_resources = []

        consumed_resources = set(r_temp[r_temp > 0])
        for res in consumed_resources:
            res_idx = res - 1
            Rs_r = Rs_cur[res_idx]
            if Rs_r <= 0:
                continue

            # Indices of species consuming this resource
            consumers = np.where(r_temp == res)[0]
            # Species with positive growth rates drive depletion
            pos_consumers = consumers[g_temp[consumers] > 0]

            if len(pos_consumers) == 0:
                # No positive-growth consumers: resource never depletes this interval
                continue

            # f(dt) = sum n_i*(exp(g_i*dt)-1) - Rs_r = 0
            def f(dt, consumers=pos_consumers, n=spc_cur, gr=g_temp, Rs_r=Rs_r):
                return np.sum(n[consumers] * (np.exp(gr[consumers] * dt) - 1)) - Rs_r

            # Check if resource will be depleted within remaining time
            f_at_T = f(t_remaining)
            if f_at_T <= 0:
                # Resource not fully consumed in remaining time
                continue

            # Find root in (0, t_remaining)
            f_at_0 = f(0.0)  # should be -Rs_r < 0
            try:
                sol = root_scalar(f, bracket=[0.0, t_remaining], method='brentq')
                t_dep = sol.root
            except Exception:
                continue

            if t_dep < min_dt:
                min_dt = t_dep
                depleting_resources = [res]
            elif abs(t_dep - min_dt) < 1e-12:
                depleting_resources.append(res)

        # Advance the system by min_dt
        dt = min_dt

        # Update species abundances (exponential growth)
        for i in range(N):
            if alive[i] and r_temp[i] > 0:
                spc_cur[i] = spc_cur[i] * exp(g_temp[i] * dt)

        # Update resource levels: consumed = new biomass produced
        for res in consumed_resources:
            res_idx = res - 1
            consumers = np.where(r_temp == res)[0]
            consumed = np.sum(
                spc_init_interval := spc_cur[consumers] / np.where(g_temp[consumers] != 0,
                                                                     np.exp(g_temp[consumers] * dt),
                                                                     1.0),
                # Back-compute initial abundance for this interval
            )
            # Recompute properly: consumed = sum n_i(0_interval) * (exp(g_i*dt) - 1)
            # n_i(0_interval) = spc_cur[i] / exp(g_i*dt)  [since we already updated spc_cur above]
            n_start = spc_cur[consumers] / np.where(g_temp[consumers] != 0,
                                                     np.exp(g_temp[consumers] * dt),
                                                     np.ones(len(consumers)))
            delta = np.sum(n_start * (np.exp(g_temp[consumers] * dt) - 1))
            Rs_cur[res_idx] = max(0.0, Rs_cur[res_idx] - delta)

        # Set depleting resources exactly to zero
        for res in depleting_resources:
            Rs_cur[res - 1] = 0.0

        t_elapsed += dt

        # Safety: if dt is essentially zero, break to avoid infinite loop
        if dt < 1e-15:
            break

    spc_end = spc_cur
    Rs_end = Rs_cur

    return spc_end, Rs_end

# Background: In serial-dilution batch-culture experiments (Goyal/Maslov/Posfai hierarchical
# consumer-resource framework), a microbial community is propagated through many repeated
# growth-and-dilution cycles. Each cycle proceeds as follows:
#
# 1. GROWTH PHASE: Species grow exponentially for time T, consuming resources according to their
#    strict preference ordering (Liebig sequential-utilization rule), via OneCycle. Resources
#    are finite and depletable; species switch to next-preferred resources as each is exhausted.
#
# 2. DILUTION: At cycle end, a fraction D of the culture is transferred to a fresh vessel.
#    Both species abundances and leftover resources are multiplied by D (0 < D < 1):
#      spc_new = D * spc_end
#      Rs_leftover = D * Rs_end
#    This models the physical serial-transfer step (e.g., 1:100 dilution → D = 0.01).
#
# 3. RESOURCE REPLENISHMENT: Fresh growth medium is added to the diluted transfer. The fresh
#    chunk equals the original initial resource array Rs (same every cycle), so:
#      Rs_cur = D * Rs_end + Rs_init
#    This resets resources to near-initial levels each cycle (if fully consumed, Rs_end ≈ 0,
#    so Rs_cur ≈ Rs_init).
#
# 4. EXTINCTION THRESHOLDING: Species whose post-dilution abundance falls below SPC_THRES
#    are declared extinct and set to exactly 0. This prevents numerical ghost populations
#    from persisting indefinitely and influencing resource consumption in later cycles.
#
# After N_cycles, any species with abundance > 0 (i.e., above the threshold, since
# sub-threshold species are zeroed) is a survivor. Competitive exclusion theory (R* rule
# and its hierarchical generalizations) predicts at most R coexisting species for R resources
# under strict preference ordering; serial dilution drives the community toward this attractor.

def SimulatedCycles(g, pref, spc_init, Rs, SPC_THRES, T, D, N_cycles):
    '''This function simulates multiple dilution cycles and return the survivors
    Inputs:
    g: growth matrix of species i on resource j. 2d float numpy array of size (N, R). 
    pref: species' preference order, 2d numpy array with dimensions [N, R] and int elements between 1 and R
    spc_init: species abundance at the beginning of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length N. 
    Rs: resource level in environment at the beginning of cycle. 1d float numpy array of length R. 
    SPC_THRES: species dieout cutoff, float
    T: time span of dilution cycle. float. 
    D: dilution rate, float
    N_cycles: number of dilution cycles, int. 
    Outputs: 
    survivors: list of surviving species, elements are integers
    '''

    Rs_init = Rs.copy().astype(float)

    # Initialize current state
    spc_cur = spc_init.copy().astype(float)
    Rs_cur = Rs_init.copy()

    # Apply extinction threshold to initial population
    spc_cur[spc_cur < SPC_THRES] = 0.0

    for cycle in range(N_cycles):
        # Growth phase: run one dilution cycle
        spc_end, Rs_end = OneCycle(g, pref, spc_cur, Rs_cur, T)

        # Dilution: keep fraction D of species and leftover resources
        spc_cur = D * spc_end

        # Replenishment: add fresh resource chunk (same as initial Rs every cycle)
        Rs_cur = D * Rs_end + Rs_init

        # Extinction thresholding: species below threshold are declared extinct
        spc_cur[spc_cur < SPC_THRES] = 0.0

    # Survivors: species with abundance > 0 (sub-threshold species already zeroed)
    survivors = [idx for idx, abundance in enumerate(spc_cur) if abundance > 0]

    return survivors
